html:
VI

EdouardвАЩs Journal: Madame Molinier

Those novelists deceive us who show the individualвАЩs development without taking into account the pressure of surroundings. The forest fashions the tree. To each one how small a place is given! How many buds are atrophied! One shoots oneвАЩs branches where one can. The mystic bough is due more often than not to stifling. The only escape is upwards. I cannot understand how Pauline manages not to grow a mystic bough, nor what further pressure she needs. She has talked to me more intimately than ever before. I did not suspect, I confess, the amount of disillusionment and resignation she hides beneath the appearance of happiness. But I recognize that she would have had to have a very vulgar nature not to have been disappointed in Molinier. In my conversation with her the day before yesterday, I was able to gauge his limits. How in the world could Pauline have married him?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Alas! the most lamentable lack of allвБ†вАФlack of characterвБ†вАФis a hidden one, to be revealed only by time and usage.

Pauline puts all her efforts into palliating OscarвАЩs insufficiencies and weaknesses, into hiding them from everyone; and especially from his children. Her utmost ingenuity is employed in enabling them to respect their father; and she is really hard put to it; but she does it in such a way that I myself was deceived. She speaks of her husband without contempt, but with a kind of indulgence which is expressive enough. She deplores his want of authority over the boys; and, as I expressed my regrets at OlivierвАЩs being with Passavant, I understood that if it had depended on her, the trip to Corsica would not have taken place.

вАЬI didnвАЩt approve of it,вАЭ she said, вАЬand to tell you the truth, I donвАЩt much care about that Monsieur Passavant. But what could I do? When I see that I canвАЩt prevent a thing, I prefer granting it with a good grace. As for Oscar, he always gives in; he gives in to me, too. But when I think itвАЩs my duty to oppose any plan of the childrenвАЩsвБ†вАФstand out against them in any way, he never supports me in the least. On this occasion Vincent stepped in as well. After that, how could I oppose Olivier without risking the loss of his confidence? And itвАЩs that I care about most.вАЭ

She was darning old socksвБ†вАФthe socks, I said to myself, which were no longer good enough for Olivier. She stopped to thread her needle, and then went on again in a lower voice, more confidingly and more sadly:

вАЬHis confidence.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ If I were only sure I still had it. But no; IвАЩve lost it.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

The protest I attemptedвБ†вАФwithout convictionвБ†вАФmade her smile. She dropped her work and went on:

вАЬFor instance, I know he is in Paris. George met him this morning; he mentioned it casually, and I pretended not to hear, for I donвАЩt like him to tell tales about his brother. But still I know it. Olivier hides things from me. When I see him again, he will think himself obliged to lie to me, and I shall pretend to believe him, as I pretend to believe his father every time he hides things from me.вАЭ

вАЬItвАЩs for fear of paining you.вАЭ

вАЬHe pains me a great deal more as it is. I am not intolerant. There are a number of little shortcomings that I tolerate, that I shut my eyes to.вАЭ

вАЬOf whom are you talking now?вАЭ

вАЬOh! of the father as well as the sons.вАЭ

вАЬWhen you pretend not to see them, you are lying too.вАЭ

вАЬBut what am I to do? ItвАЩs enough not to complain. I really canвАЩt approve! No, I say to myself that, sooner or later, one loses hold, that the tenderest affection is helpless. More than that. ItвАЩs in the way; itвАЩs a nuisance. I have come to the pitch of hiding my love itself.вАЭ

вАЬNow you are talking of your sons.вАЭ

вАЬWhy do you say that? Do you mean that I canвАЩt love Oscar any more? Sometimes I think so, but I think too that itвАЩs for fear of suffering too much that I donвАЩt love him more. And.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, I suppose you are rightвБ†вАФin OlivierвАЩs case, I prefer to suffer.вАЭ

вАЬAnd Vincent?вАЭ

вАЬA few years ago everything I now say of Olivier would have been true of Vincent.вАЭ

вАЬMy poor friend.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Soon you will be saying the same of George.вАЭ

вАЬBut one becomes resigned, slowly. And yet one didnвАЩt ask so much of life. One learns to ask lessвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ less and less.вАЭ Then she added softly: вАЬAnd of oneself, more and more.вАЭ

вАЬWith ideas of that kind, one is almost a Christian,вАЭ said I, smiling in my turn.

вАЬI sometimes think so too. But having them isnвАЩt enough to make one a Christian.вАЭ

вАЬAny more than being a Christian is enough to make one have them.вАЭ

вАЬI have often thoughtвБ†вАФwill you let me say so?вБ†вАФthat in their fatherвАЩs default, you might speak to the boys.вАЭ

вАЬVincent is not here.вАЭ

вАЬIt is too late for him. I am thinking of Olivier. ItвАЩs with you that I should have liked him to go away.вАЭ

At these words, which gave me the sudden imagination of what might have been if I had not so thoughtlessly listened to the appeal of passing adventure, a dreadful emotion wrung my heart, and at first I could find nothing to say; then, as the tears started to my eyes, and wishing to give some appearance of a motive to my disturbance:

вАЬToo late, I fear, for him too,вАЭ I sighed.

Pauline seized my hand:

вАЬHow good you are!вАЭ she cried.

Embarrassed at seeing her thus mistake me, and unable to undeceive her, I could only turn aside the conversation from a subject which put me too ill at my ease.

вАЬAnd George?вАЭ I asked.

вАЬHe makes me more anxious than the other two put together,вАЭ she answered. вАЬI canвАЩt say that with him I am losing my hold, for he has never been either confiding or obedient.вАЭ

She hesitated a few moments. It obviously cost her a great deal to say what follows.

вАЬThis summer something very serious happened,вАЭ she went on at last, вАЬsomething itвАЩs a little painful for me to speak to you about, especially as I am still not very sure.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ A hundred-franc note disappeared from a cupboard in which I was in the habit of keeping my money. The fear of being wrong in my suspicions prevented me from bringing any accusation; the maid who waited on us at the hotel was a very young girl and seemed to me honest. I said I had lost the note before George; I might as well admit that my suspicions fell upon him. He didnвАЩt appear disturbed; he didnвАЩt blush.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I felt ashamed of having suspected him; I tried to persuade myself I had made a mistake. I did my accounts over again; unfortunately there was no possibility of a doubtвБ†вАФa hundred francs were missing. I shrank from questioning him, and finally I didnвАЩt. The fear of seeing him add a lie to a theft kept me back. Was I wrong?вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Yes, I reproach myself now for not having insisted; perhaps it was out of a fear that I should have to be too severeвБ†вАФor that I shouldnвАЩt be severe enough. Once again, I played the part of a person who knows nothing, but with a very anxious heart, I assure you. I had let the time go by, and I said to myself it was too late and that the punishment would come too long after the fault. And how punish him? I did nothing; I reproach myself for itвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ but what could I have done?

вАЬI had thought of sending him to England; I even wanted to ask your advice about it, but I didnвАЩt know where you were.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ At any rate, I didnвАЩt hide my trouble from himвБ†вАФmy anxiety; I think he must have felt it, for, you know, he has a good heart. I count more on his own conscience to reproach him than on anything I could have said. He wonвАЩt do it again, I feel certain. He used to go about with a very rich boy at the seaside, and he was no doubt led on to spend money. No doubt I must have left the cupboard open; and I repeat, IвАЩm not really sure it was he. There were a great many people coming and going in the hotel.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶вАЭ

I admired the ingenious way in which she put forward every possible consideration that might exonerate her child.

вАЬI should have liked him to put the money back,вАЭ I said.

вАЬI hoped he would. And when he didnвАЩt, I thought it must be a proof of his innocence. And then I said to myself that he was afraid to.вАЭ

вАЬDid you tell his father?вАЭ

She hesitated a few moments:

вАЬNo,вАЭ she said at last, вАЬI prefer him to know nothing about it.вАЭ

No doubt she thought she heard a noise in the next room; she went to make sure there was no one there; then she sat down again beside me.

вАЬOscar told me you lunched together the other day. He was so loud in your praise, that I suppose what you chiefly did was to listen to him.вАЭ (She smiled sadly, as she said these words.) вАЬIf he confided in you, I have no desire not to respect his confidencesвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ though in reality I know a great deal more about his private life than he imagines. But since I got back, I canвАЩt understand what has come over him. He is so gentleвБ†вАФI was almost going to sayвБ†вАФso humble.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ ItвАЩs almost embarrassing. He goes on as if he were afraid of me. He neednвАЩt be. For a long time past IвАЩve been aware that he has been carrying on.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ I even know with whom. He thinks I know nothing about it and takes enormous pains to hide it; but his precautions are so obvious, that the more he hides, the more he gives himself away. Every time he goes out with an affectation of being busy, worried, anxious, I know that he is off to his pleasure. I feel inclined to say to him: вАШBut, my dear friend, IвАЩm not keeping you; are you afraid IвАЩm jealous?вАЩ I should laugh if I had the heart to. My only fear is that the children may notice something; heвАЩs so carelessвБ†вАФso clumsy! Sometimes, without his suspecting it, I find myself forced to help him, as if I were playing his game. I assure you I end by being almost amused by it; I invent excuses for him; I put the letters he leaves lying about back in his coat pocket.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs just it,вАЭ I said; вАЬheвАЩs afraid you have discovered some letters.вАЭ

вАЬDid he tell you so?вАЭ

вАЬAnd thatвАЩs whatвАЩs making him so nervous.вАЭ

вАЬDo you think I want to read them?вАЭ

A kind of wounded pride made her draw herself up. I was obliged to add:

вАЬItвАЩs not a question of the letters he may have mislaid inadvertently; but of some letters he had put in a drawer and which he says he canвАЩt find. He thinks you have taken them.вАЭ

At these words, I saw Pauline turn pale, and the horrible suspicion which darted upon her, forced itself suddenly into my mind too. I regretted having spoken, but it was too late. She looked away from me and murmured:

вАЬWould to Heaven it were I!вАЭ

She seemed overcome.

вАЬWhat am I to do?вАЭ she repeated. вАЬWhat am I to do?вАЭ Then raising her eyes to mine again: вАЬYou? CouldnвАЩt you speak to him?вАЭ

Although she avoided, as I did, pronouncing GeorgeвАЩs name, it was clear that she was thinking of him.

вАЬI will try. I will think it over,вАЭ I said, rising. And as she accompanied me to the front door:

вАЬSay nothing about it to Oscar, please. Let him go on suspecting meвБ†вАФthinking what he thinks.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ It is better so. Come and see me again.вАЭ
